trajectory_summary: The call opens cold, briefly cracks open at 00:40 when Charles grants permission to proceed, then closes immediately when Meg exits without attempting a single probe or reframe. The trajectory is a straight line from cold to closed — no energy was built at any point. The outcome is a self-inflicted early exit on a lead that contained at least one actionable probe opportunity (the bill-equivalence moment) and one exploratory question worth asking (the slate roof age).
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is listed as 'unknown' — based on the transcript, the correct tag is 'DQ / no appointment.' However, the DQ is rep-initiated, not customer-initiated. Charles never explicitly refused to continue; Meg offered the exit and Charles accepted it. A more accurate tag would be 'premature_exit' or 'rep_self_DQ' to distinguish this from a genuine customer refusal.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Objection inversion on customer's own bill number
primary_coaching_focus.quote: you're gonna pay a 150, $200 a month, which is, you know, what I pay in electricity now
primary_coaching_focus.why: Charles handed Meg his current bill and framed it as a reason solar doesn't work — which is the exact setup for objection_inversion. Meg said 'Mhmm' and moved on. This is the highest-leverage missed move in the call: the flip requires no fabricated numbers, uses only what Charles said, and is the one technique that could have broken his five-year-old framing. Until Meg can execute this move on a live call, she will exit every call where the customer has a formed opinion.

key_moments.stewart_read: Charles has vented his skepticism and then explicitly grants Meg the floor. This is the functional equivalent of the hello-objection moment — the customer has aired resistance and is now, however grudgingly, inviting a response. The protocol calls for a small ask: acknowledge the framing, ask for 30 seconds to make the case, and if it's not relevant, leave him alone. Meg instead reflected his skepticism back as a closed question ('so it sounds like you're not really looking at solar options for the home. Right?'), which invited him to confirm his own disinterest. Strategic read: Meg may have been trying to clarify before pitching. Reactive read: she pattern-matched to 'not interested' and began her exit sequence one full minute early. The reactive read is better supported — her next substantive move is the exit at 01:52.

key_moments.quote: I thought that's what I was gonna get, but all I'm gonna get is a sales pitch. I got you.
key_moments.stewart_read: Charles is not expressing scam anxiety in the traditional door-knocker sense, but he is expressing form-fill regret — he believes the lead-gen mechanism misrepresented what he was signing up for. This is a legitimacy gap, not a product objection. The correct move is honest specificity (per protocols.how_did_you_get_my_number) plus an acknowledgment that his frustration is reasonable, followed by a reframe: 'You're right that the form led you here — and I'm not going to pretend otherwise. But since you're already on the phone, can I ask you one question about what you found in your research?' Meg did not address the form-fill complaint at all.

key_moments.quote: you're gonna pay a 150, $200 a month, which is, you know, what I pay in electricity now
key_moments.stewart_read: Charles hands Meg his current bill range and frames it as a reason solar doesn't work. This is the objection_inversion entry point: his own number is the leverage. The flip available here is not a fabricated savings claim — it's a reframe of his own math: 'So you're already paying $150-200 a month to the utility. The question isn't whether you pay — you're already paying. The question is whether you keep paying them or start paying yourself.' Meg said 'Mhmm' and let the moment pass entirely.

key_moments.quote: I have a, a slate roof. And so you you can't put you can't put solar panels on a slate roof.
key_moments.stewart_read: Charles presents the slate roof as a self-DQ. This may be accurate — slate roofs are a legitimate installation challenge — but Charles's information is five years old by his own admission ('it was five years ago'). The probe worth attempting before accepting the DQ is: 'How old is the roof?' A slate roof nearing end-of-life may be a replacement candidate, which opens a different conversation. This is not a clean cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned trigger — Charles expressed no replacement intent — but it is a speculative probe opportunity: explore before accepting the DQ. Meg accepted the DQ without asking.
